conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start by reading the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To get started, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to claim Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 17 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have a son, Alex, who is 17 years old and lives with you at home.

Since Alex is 17, I need to ask: **Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

(This is important because Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19 if they remain in approved education or training.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he is. Alex is still in sixth form doing his A-levels.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great! Since Alex is 17 and in sixth form doing his A-levels, that counts as approved education.

Now I need to ask: **Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you for confirming that.

Just a few more questions to complete the assessment:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information.

One final question: **Does Alex work, and if so, how many hours per week does he work?**

(This is important because if a child works 24 hours or more per week and is no longer in approved education or training, it can affect eligibility - though in Alex's case, he is still in education.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he does work. He has a part-time job at the supermarket - he works full-time hours there, so that's about 24 hours a week or more, but he's still doing his A-levels alongside it.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit based on what you've told me.

Based on the official guidance I've reviewed, I need to carefully consider Alex's situation. According to the rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child "starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training."

In Alex's case:
- He is 17 years old
- He lives with you in the UK
- He is still in approved education (doing A-levels in sixth form)
- He works 24 hours or more per week at a supermarket
- Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him

The key factor here is that Alex **is still in approved education or training** (his A-levels). The rule states that Child Benefit stops if a child works 24 hours or more a week **and** is no longer in approved education. Since Alex is continuing with his A-levels, he remains in approved education, which means you should still be eligible for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Even though Alex works 24 hours or more per week, he is still eligible because he remains in approved education (his A-levels). The rule only disqualifies children who work 24+ hours per week **and** are no longer in education or training. Since Alex is continuing with his sixth form studies, you meet all the eligibility criteria.

You can proceed with your Child Benefit claim. Just remember that:
- Child Benefit will continue as long as Alex remains in approved education or training (until he turns 20)
- If he leaves education or gets certain benefits in his own right, you'll need to notify HMRC
- If you or your partner earn over £60,000 individually, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge

Is there anything else you'd like to know about your Child Benefit claim?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and lives at home with the claimant in the UK. According to the eligibility rules, Child Benefit continues for children aged 16-19 if they stay in approved education or training. Alex is currently in sixth form doing his A-levels, which counts as approved education. While Alex works 24 hours or more per week at a supermarket, the rule states that Child Benefit stops only if the child 'starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week AND is no longer in approved education or training.' Since Alex is still in approved education (A-levels), this disqualification does not apply. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. The claimant lives in the UK and is responsible for Alex who lives with them. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 17-year-old son Alex. Although Alex works 24 hours or more per week, he remains in approved education (sixth form A-levels), which means the work hours do not disqualify the claim. All other eligibility criteria are met: the claimant lives in the UK, Alex lives with them, no one else is claiming for Alex, and Alex is under 20 and in approved education.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and lives at home with the claimant in the UK. According to the eligibility rules, Child Benefit continues for children aged 16-19 if they stay in approved education or training. Alex is currently in sixth form doing his A-levels, which counts as approved education. While Alex works 24 hours or more per week at a supermarket, the rule states that Child Benefit stops only if the child 'starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week AND is no longer in approved education or training.' Since Alex is still in approved education (A-levels), this disqualification does not apply. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. The claimant lives in the UK and is responsible for Alex who lives with them. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 17-year-old son Alex. Although Alex works 24 hours or more per week, he remains in approved education (sixth form A-levels), which means the work hours do not disqualify the claim. All other eligibility criteria are met: the claimant lives in the UK, Alex lives with them, no one else is claiming for Alex, and Alex is under 20 and in approved education.
